speaker: interviewer
text: 1995. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] We left off on the last take talking about what officially was done, some anti-Jewish practices, such as boycotts. Now I want to ask you, when did you begin to notice changes? What started occurring?

speaker: interviewee
text: You mean before the war?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, I mean-- yeah. What gradual changes did you notice?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. In the contrary, this was an ongoing--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was ongoing.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Situation.

speaker: interviewer
text: But then when did it abruptly change?

speaker: interviewee
text: It never really changed. Things have changed because of the war. So instead of the Poles, the Germans took over. But there was no change coming from the Poles with regards to their feelings of anti-Semitism.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the days-- the date the Germans occupied Krakow.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, as we all know, the war began September 1, 1939. It was September 6 when they reached Krakow. And they occupied it without a shot.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you doing that day? Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a day-- September 6 was already-- we were already 6 days in the war. And we were expecting the-- the entrance of the Germans any minute. And it came in the morning of September 6. Well, we were, of course, then confined to house because we were afraid. We didn't know what to expect when the Germans marched in. We knew the reputation of the Germans, what they did to Jews. But, of course, we could not imagine at that time what will come for the Jews. But regardless, we were just cautious, looked out the windows. And we saw the Germans with the tanks and with the carriers and the infantry coming in and getting into-- first of all, they occupied the government buildings. And it just happens like it would happen in any war when an occupation army comes into a city, into a town. And for the first few days, we just ventured out to the street here and there. But it was very, with very great hesitation. But slowly and gradually, we got used to this. And we started to resume a normal-- began to resume a normal life under the circumstances. And in fact, some thoughts came to our minds at that time saying the Germans cannot be that bad. Look, they don't kill us. They're here already for two days, for three days, for a week. But soon, things did start happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were living with your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was living with my parents.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were not with any, your wife at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not married.

speaker: interviewer
text: You hadn't met her yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I met my wife before the war, but I was not married.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not married at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at the very beginning, after maybe a week or two, we-- all kinds of ordinances appeared.

speaker: interviewer
text: What ordinances?

speaker: interviewee
text: With regards to Jews. First of all, the first order was that the Jews have to be identified by wearing the star of David on their arm-- on their left arm. Then the order came that all Jewish stores and establishments have to be identified with a large star of David at the entrance, so that they knew right then which and where is a Jewish establishment and who is a Jew in the street. Because unlike the Poles, the Germans wouldn't be able to identify a Jew when you see him in the street. The Poles could differentiate between a Jew and a non-Jew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they needed identification. And as soon as we were wearing these armbands, things started to happen in the streets. They were rounding up the Jews, putting them on trucks, and taking them someplace to do some work, some manual work. Mostly cleaning work in the street work or cleaning up buildings, or this type of work. What did you get assigned to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, if I was caught. This was not an assignment. At this time there was no requirement to report for work. And this came very soon after this. But at the beginning, it was just-- they were rounding up people with armbands and taking them away on trucks, and then they let him go, and they could return home in the evenings after they were through.

speaker: interviewer
text: So where were you? Were you managing to evade them or?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, I was managing to evade them most of the time, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: By being00 avoiding the streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you stayed inside the house most of the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, right. This was the only way you could really evade them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How does it feel for you having to wear the armband? What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't analyze it to be truthful. When you look at this from a different perspective, and you see yourself being identified out of everybody else to show that you are you, this might be a tremendous-- a traumatic experience. But I personally didn't analyze that. And I didn't give it much thought. This is what-- it's weird. Took it in stride.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as long as you remained inside your home, you were OK for the time being. Is that correct?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it is correct. Then they gave all this to the municipality. The Jews had their own municipality, their own, what do they call it, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] like a congregation. And there was a president and there was a staff. And then the Germans used to put in orders to this-- that they would supply some people. Then it became different. Then it was not a random round up in the street. But it was you had already responsibility. The people in charge of the city, of the Jewish life, had to provide certain number of people for work every day. So then everybody was already involved because they were calling people to-- to report for work that day or that day and so on. Then it became more organized slave labor. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Very important thing happened in December-- December 4 and 5 in Krakow. This is 1949-- I mean '39 I'm talking about. In the morning of December 4, the German surrounded the quarters where most of the Jews lived, [NON-ENGLISH], what it's called. It used to be the Jewish ghetto. At this time in Poland, it was nothing. It was just a quarters where the Jewish lived together. Like today they live in Williamsburg. And they surrounded this whole section. And nobody could get out of the house. We didn't know what to expect. Rumors started to fly right away that they are going in, they're raping people, and they're killing people. It turned out to be only half true. There were cases instance of rape and shootings, yes. But in general, the purpose of this was just to plunder the Jews, their wealth. Just to take away their possessions, their valuables.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they come to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: They came to every house.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did they take from your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Everything that they thought is useful for them. We're talking about silver and valuable because every Jewish family has got a silver candelabra or candlesticks and this and other pieces of silver. Or if somebody had some crystals. This goes without saying, they took it all away. But they also took away personal things. They took away my shoes and my good suits. And this was not an official thing. This was on the basis of the individual. He liked it, he took it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when they came in? Where were you? Just standing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just just being in the house. The whole family was there. And they were-- they used to intimidate you by making this terrible noise when they used to come in just, you know, the noise alone could kill you.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of noises?

speaker: interviewee
text: Noise. [SPEAKING GERMAN]. This is you damn Jews. And this was their entrance. And then they said-- they asked to produce whatever valuables we have and foreign currency-- gold, silver, and foreign currency. And then whatever we =d, they were not satisfied with that. They were looking around. They were opening doors. They were opening drawers. And throwing things away. Like they were plundering the house. This is what they did from house to house for two days. Some people died from just from being scared. They got heart attacks. And some people got killed by them because they didn't like the way they moved, or how fast the moved. In fact, my mother-in-law, the mother of my first wife, got a stroke at that day. And a few days later, she died. And it's definitely attributable to this incidence.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you when they came? Can you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was terror. The sight of the Germans in their uniforms-- and these were very neatly dressed Germans, the SS mostly, or the [NON-ENGLISH] which is the SD which it is [SPEAKING GERMAN]. And they were very good looking young men. And very well neatly dressed. And to us, they were like monsters, because they brought with them this terror that they were projecting wherever they came, particularly in regard to Jews. Because we knew what they want from Jews. We knew their-- what their intentions are.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- so then what transpired after this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a terrible incidence that happened in December. And then life went back almost to normal under the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you working at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had they assigned you yet?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- no. We had this chocolate factory. We had some raw materials. And we started by ourselves without employing anybody to produce, to make chocolate, to make the product from it, and to be able to sustain ourselves and to sell it. And then about-- this must have been the beginning of March in 1940, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we still had this factory. We still had some raw materials to work with. Then a young German in civilian clothes came in, and he produced papers that he is going to take-- he was assigned by the German government to take over the factory. They called it [SPEAKING GERMAN], the one who was not for himself, so to speak, but he was representing the German government. And he was taking over the factory on behalf of the German government. We had absolutely nothing to say in this. He had his papers, and this was what he was going to do. Then I was talking to him, because I spoke a little German. And then I asked him where he stays, where he lives. And in the evening, I visited him. I didn't wear my armband. I took it off and tried to reason with him. And told him the following. Here, this is a chocolate factory. You are dependent on production. You're dependent on raw materials, which are not readily available in Poland. Not in any place that you are, not in Germany even. Because chocolate is produced, if you know, from cocoa beans. This is kind of a bean like coffee beans, only it's called a cocoa bean. This is the basis for production of chocolate. You roast them, and you grind them. And then you also use the butter that you extract from these cocoa beans, it's called cocoa butter. And this is, in combination with sugar, you make chocolate.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said here, you have maybe two weeks supply of raw materials. What are you going to do later? You'll be out of a job. And I gave him some money. And he said forget about it. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And I saved our factory for another few months until we are able to produce what we have, and we lived on it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So in other words, he let you continue to run it?

speaker: interviewee
text: He let me continue because he saw the logic of what I told him. So he was looking for another better job. I mean, he could get anything he wanted.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what happened after a few months? You say it continued for a few months.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then this was already in the summer of 1940. Then there came new orders that there will be a ghetto created in Krakow. And the date was set for March 1941. And those who want to stay in the ghetto would have to get permission to do so, would have to apply to the government officials to have permission to enter the ghetto. My family and I did not get permission to enter the ghetto. So we had to try to plan to go out, to move out from Krakow and to settle someplace else. There were no concentration camps yet at this time. It was the beginning of creating ghettos. There were ghettos in other cities before that, but this was the time for Krakow. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Those who did not get permission to move into the ghetto had to move out by a certain date. So I-- and my wife and I was-- I didn't mention, I was married in January 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was going to ask you about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1940, Yes. So we moved to a small town, Pilzno, which is not far from Krakow, where my wife had family. My wife's parents had their cousins and others. And they moved there, and I and my wife also were planning to move to this little town, Pilzno.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your wife's name was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Was Phyllis.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And were you able to have a regular wedding in 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we had a Jewish traditional wedding in a house. It was just a chuppa, and, yes, we had a wedding.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the ghetto was established, you moved with your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, much before that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before that.

speaker: interviewee
text: When the ghetto-- then it was too late. If you didn't have permission to get into the ghetto, you had to move out by a certain date in 1940. But towards the end, I don't remember the date exactly. And those who received permission to stay, they could stay in their homes until the ghetto was opened. And this happened in March 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you moved?

speaker: interviewee
text: I moved with my wife to Pilzno.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: And my family, the rest of my family, my parents and my brothers and sisters, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] didn't have a place to go. So I went to a small town near [NON-ENGLISH]. The name of the, actually it's a village, was named [NON-ENGLISH]. And I was able to rent a little house from a-- from a peasant. And I went back to Krakow. I went to to the railway station. I rented a box car. And I loaded most of the furniture and the belongings of my parents on that boxcar, and I brought it down to [NON-ENGLISH]. And then my parents and the rest of my family reported to the authorities that this is where they want to go because they had to leave here. And they let them go on the train. So I established them in [NON-ENGLISH], and then I went back to Pilzno with my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your life like in Pilzno?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a day by day, you had to go out to work because there was, in every little town, there was Yiddish [NON-ENGLISH], so to speak. This is a congregation which was taking care of Jewish affairs. And they had orders from the Germans to supply a workforce. And we had to go to work everyday, and wherever they send you.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they send you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Locally.

speaker: interviewer
text: To do what?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was locally. To do-- it's not that they needed the work. They wanted to humiliate the people and to put them to work. That's all. It's not to do work. Most of it was not factory work, it was not office work, it was street work.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you personally do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a broom. I swept the streets. It was not really work that they needed that you say. They wanted to keep these people occupied, and at the same time, humiliate them by giving them some work that they're never accustomed to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: So most of your time was spent sweeping the streets?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, but this was a short time. I lived in Pilzno, actually in Pilzno, it wasn't such a short time. Because by the end of 1941 is when they said Pilzno will become [SPEAKING GERMAN], free of Jews. And you have to move into a next town, which was Dembitz. And over there, there would be a Jewish ghetto. And this is where all the people living around in this area, Dembitz was one of the larger towns. And all of these people have to move into a certain date to a ghetto in Dembitz, which we did.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dembitz was how far from Krakow? Is it like a suburb of Krakow?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, it wasn't a suburb, but it was about maybe 50 miles, 40 miles, this is in that area.

speaker: interviewer
text: So Dembitz became a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Became a ghetto in Dembitz, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe what your living conditions were like there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, over there, this was already you couldn't go live, rent anything to live. You had to move into where you were assigned to. So there were-- the living quarters were very limited, and people had to join, to share rooms, apartments, you know. And I and my wife lived in this in a house that there were three rooms. There were at least six families in these three rooms.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: But why, just I and my wife? I just neglected to tell you that my parents-- my in-laws, my wife's parents, her mother died during that incident in Krakow. But then her father and two sisters, they were all shipped out from Pilzno to another destination that we didn't know. Most probably to [INAUDIBLE]. So the only ones to survive that particular incident was my wife and I. And we moved in. We came to Dembitz to move into that ghetto. So she didn't have any family anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like if you put six families in a three room apartment? You can imagine what it is like. Again, things were happening so-- you really didn't think about these things. You didn't analyze things. First of all, we were young. And we were hit over the head suddenly. [INAUDIBLE] separated from the family. All of a sudden, you're an orphan. You don't have any family anymore. And one day, you are just alone. So it's hard-- if I tell you what I thought about it, what I thought at this time, it wouldn't be the truth. I probably thought about myself. What kind of sanitary conditions existed with so many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: You cannot call it sanitary conditions. There were-- at this time, there was still outhouses there. But there were some apartments that had facilities inside. So imagine six families living in one apartment with one facility, with one bathroom. It was standing in line all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Food was only what they provided, what the Germans provided. We got ration cards. And we could get food on these ration cards.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of food did you eat there?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was mostly bread, potatoes, cabbage. This is the stuff that you would have.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this ghetto in Dembitz was it surrounded by a wall by, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not at this time. It was not. But the ghetto in Krakow was. It was not surrounded. In one spot, it had a wall and the rest was surrounded with barbed wire, Krakow ghetto. The Dembitz ghetto was not. It was like in the open, you would say. Only you would know these are the parameters of the ghetto, and this is where you can be.

speaker: interviewer
text: How large was it approximately? How many blocks would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there lived about 1500 people in there. It's a small town, small houses. You cannot go by blocks. You cannot compare blocks. But it was very, very limited. Maybe 25, 30 houses. Something like this. And this didn't last for very long. The ghetto-- one day there came orders that the ghetto is going to be reduced. In other words, you are now 1500, we are going to leave only 250 people who are essential for the German war effort. And these people were assigned certain work at the railroad. So these 250 will be able to remain in the ghetto, and the rest will be resettled. But this was a euphemism for deportation. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day in the morning we had all to assemble in the square in Dembitz with our belongings. You know, with what can you carry. What did you have with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had just a little case with my shaving [INAUDIBLE] that's all. Because by then I was already experienced that taking belongings don't mean anything, because eventually they take it away from you. So while we were assembled in that square, the ghetto commandant came with a list. And he read a list of 250, as it was said, 250 people, and he asked them to step out to the side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name of the commandant?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remembered it, but I don't remember this time. I don't. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No, I don't. So my wife was among the 250 that their names were called. And she was supposed to be able to still remain. And we knew that, because we knew according to where she was working. So we knew that she is on that list. But I was not. So she didn't want to step out. She didn't want to stay. She wanted to go with me wherever I go. But I forced her to step out from the ranks and join the others who were supposed to be left over. And I told her I will come back for you. These were my words. The rest of us were marched to the railway station. And there were-- there was a train waiting for us, a cattle train, these boxcars. And we were shoved in 100 people to a train. They were counting everybody. They didn't let anybody more than 100. They had their own ideas about things. It was all-- and once I came into that train, I saw-- I didn't have any illusions as to where the destination of that train is. Because I knew there were no facilities. There was no water. There were mixed-- mixed people, old, young, children, infants. Whoever was there from the sick people from the hospital. So I know this is no good. We are not going any place. And I walk myself to the corner. These cattle cars have corners-- have small windows about two by three feet in every corner. And I worked myself to be close to that corner. And they were-- the windows were crisscrossed with barbed wire so that Nobody could escape. But at least I wanted to be at the window. And when the train-- and we were standing there on the platform for maybe 5, 6 hours. And towards the evening, the train started moving. And there were other young people waiting also in that corner. And we started to talk that we would like to conceive some kind of a plan how to escape that train. And we actually, we have managed to remove that barbed wire from the windows.
